
## Blog listings

- [ ] User interface
- [ ] Images (first image of the post or custom image in the front matter)
- [ ] Date formatting
- [ ] Sorting (by date, by title, etc.)
- [ ] Pagination
- [ ] Breadcrumbs are not clickable and show the full path rather /posts/datatable_nesting/
- [ ] backticks in listings titles still not rendered as markdown monospace `data.table` vs. `base` vs. `dplyr`
- [ ] vab: panel_heterogeneous_effects: pre-existing problem with dependency. Delete post?
- [ ] vab: update declare_design with new syntax
- [ ] inspect each pages


## arelbundockcom

- [ ] review all blog posts


## Slides


- [ ] reveal.js
- [ ] slidev


## calepin new

- [ ] Built-in website template
- [ ] Built-in minimal website template
- [ ] Built-in blog template
- [ ] --variant


## Plugins

- [ ] Stable template API is too large: exposing too many template variables as public API locks in internal rendering details
- [ ] How to register new plugins? What are they allowed to do?
- [ ] ANSI colors in txtfmt plugin
- [ ] CSL helpers: https://github.com/citation-style-language/styles/
- [ ] Test lightbox


## TOML

- [ ] Collect all hard-coded values from the rust code
- [ ] Transfer everythin we can to .toml
- [ ] Formal categories: content, content_standalone, what else?


## calepin new

- [ ] site
    - [ ] website (calepin)
    - [ ] simple (arelbundock.com)
    - [ ] single (litedown)
    - [ ] blog
- [ ] ebook
    - [ ] book
- [ ] document
- [ ] book


## Other formats

- [ ] docx should be more powerful and maybe not go through pandoc
- [ ] ebook?


## HTML

- [ ] footnotes should have hover


## Internal

- [ ] Performance profiling
- [ ] Architecture refactor / DRY / simplification
- [ ] User input validation. Brainstorm with AI to be super strict and informative. Paths, formats, front matter options, etc.
- [ ] Minimize dependencies
- [x] run_pagefind() should be specified as a more generic post-processing step that can be specified by the user.
- [ ] cargo-dist
    - [ ] homebrew
    - [ ] linux
    - [ ] windows
- [ ] Delegate to templates as much as possible (again and again, don't check as done until the very end)


## Manual code review

- [ ] Everything


## Paths again

- [ ] Detect
- [ ] Brainstorm
- [ ] PATH CHECK FOR SINGLE typst file
- [ ] Manual checks


# Docs


## Technical

- [ ] Table with a list of all variables supported in the different .toml blocks


## Terminology

- [ ] Calepin Terminology intro
- [ ] HTML sales pitch: classless CSS, standalone files by default
- [ ] LLM-friendly
- [ ] Performance page with `bench/rubbish.sh`
- [ ] Accessibility: 
    - [x] HTML: aria codes, skip to content, etc.
    - [ ] PDF: ???
    - [ ] Automatic alt-text for generated figures?


## Aesthetics

- [ ] *Calepin* always in italics with capital letter in website/


## Philosophy

- Content vs. Container vs. Appearance
    - Content: .qmd files
    - Container: .toml files
    - Appearance: templates and themes (css, html, latex, typst)
- [ ] High performance
- [ ] Simplicity
    - [ ] Classless HTML with simple CSS.
    - [ ] Minimal preambles in LaTeX and Typst, with optional themes
- [ ] Templates everywhere
- [ ] LLM-friendly
- [ ] Robust configuration in TOML
- [ ] Compatibility layer with Quarto (though limited)


## Evangelism

- [ ] arelbundock.com
- [ ] countrycode
- [ ] tinytable
- [ ] modelsummary
- [ ] marginaleffects


# Later

- [ ] Lightning CSS
- [ ] Code annotations
- [ ] Code numbering
- [ ] Tables: Caption location in different output formats.
- [ ] Appendix A (lettered)
    - [ ] latex
    - [ ] html
    - [ ] markdown
    - [ ] typst
- [ ] Language / localization 
    - [ ] dates everywhere relevant
    - [ ] what else?
- [ ] `fig-subcap`: Multiple figures from a single code chunk.
- [ ] Column option for code output placement (`column: margin`, `column: page`, etc.)
- [ ] Chunk labels should not add a fig- or a tbl- automatically if that is already in the label specified in ```{lang, fig-blahblah}. Otherwise we get duplicated fig-fig-blahblah.
- [ ] Custom cross-reference types.


## Typst

- [ ] Check if math works
- [ ] Improve math conversion AST + conversion


## Layout

- [ ] Column classes (`.column-body`, `.column-page`, `.column-screen`, `.column-margin`)
- [ ] Outset variants (`.column-body-outset`, `.column-page-inset`)
- [ ] Left/right overflow (`.column-screen-left`, `.column-page-right`, etc.)
- [ ] Margin content (`.column-margin` for text, figures, tables)
- [ ] Margin references (`reference-location: margin`)
- [ ] Margin citations (`citation-location: margin`)
- [ ] Margin captions (`cap-location: margin`, `fig-cap-location: margin`)
- [ ] PDF page geometry options (`geometry:` front matter)
- [ ] Screen inset shaded (`column: screen-inset-shaded`)
- [ ] Accessibility
    - [x] html + css
    - [ ] typst
    - [ ] latex
    - [ ] pdf
- [ ] PDF metadata should be optional (author, title, etc.)


## Quarto compatibility layer

- [ ] Decide on a minimal set of supported Quarto preamble features
- [ ] YAML to TOML translation layer
- [ ] There's lots of leftover there. Figure out what to extract and how to make a robust conversion layer.



## Watch


  What the watch system already does

  The watch system is file-change-driven, not hash-driven. It works like this:

  1. notify watches the filesystem for modify/create events on .qmd files
  2. On change, it collects the paths that changed (with 100ms debounce)
  3. For single-file preview: re-renders the entire file from scratch
  4. For collection preview: rebuild_documents matches changed paths against discovered pages, then only re-renders those specific pages

  This is already the right granularity for incremental rebuilds -- you only re-render what changed. The watcher is reasonably robust (debouncing, canonicalized path matching, Ctrl+C handling).

  Where hashing would add value (and where it wouldn't)

  It wouldn't help the watch case. The watcher already knows which files changed because the OS told it. Hashing the file content before deciding to re-render would add overhead for almost no benefit -- if the file was touched, you almost certainly
  want to re-render it.

  It would help make docs / calepin --batch (cold builds). Today, a full collection build re-renders every page even if nothing changed since the last build. A content hash cache could skip pages whose source (and config/templates) haven't changed.
  But this requires:

  - Hashing not just the .qmd content but everything that affects output: YAML config, shared partials, _calepin/ overrides, bibliography files, code chunk outputs
  - Invalidating correctly when a cross-reference target changes (page A references page B's figure -- if B changes, A's cross-refs may need updating)
  - Storing the cache somewhere (.cache/ directory with hash-keyed entries)

  The dependency graph problem is the hard part. Zensical sidesteps it because its pages are mostly independent (no cross-references between pages). Calepin's cross-references, shared bibliography, and code chunk dependencies make the invalidation
  surface much larger.

  What's actually worth doing

  The low-hanging fruit is what you already have: the watcher-based incremental rebuild for collections. For make docs, the most practical improvement would be skipping unchanged standalone pages (ones with no cross-references to other pages). That's
  a simple file-mtime or content-hash check without needing a full dependency graph. Pages with cross-references would always re-render, which is the safe default.
